content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 12, 2003

Teresa Vig Rein
Rein & Rein
715 14th Street
Modesto, CA 95354

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. 1-03-266

Dear Ms. Rein:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of the Stanislaus Economic Development and Workforce Alliance ("Alliance") for advice regarding the conflict of interest code provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ You have asked that we review the draft conflict of interest code you have prepared for the Alliance. Although we may provide assistance under section 87312 upon request, please note that any suggestions are not binding on Stanislaus County or on the board of supervisors as the county's code reviewing body.

BACKGROUND²

The Stanislaus Economic Development and Workforce Alliance is a nonprofit corporation with a board comprised of about 50 members. The Alliance receives federal funds through the County of Stanislaus and is a grant/service provider relating to workforce development performing, among other things, government functions relating to duties carried on by local workforce investment boards. The Alliance performs two key functions: 1) compliance with the Workforce Investment Act of 1998 ("WIA"), with funding from the federal WIA program through the WIA fiscal agent, Stanislaus County; and 2) economic development activities, including business attraction, retention and marketing, with funding from local government and private businesses. One of the main

________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² The facts of this letter are from your request for advice dated November 10, 2003, and your previous request for advice answered in Rein Advice Letter, No. A-02-124.

File No. 1-03-266
Page No. 2

functions of the Alliance will be to serve as a workforce investment board pursuant to the
WIA. For example, the Alliance will designate or procure "one stop operators" for the
workforce area and will negotiate and execute "Memoranda of Understanding" with the
required one-stop partners, collectively or severally, to provide services in the workforce
delivery system. Additionally, the Alliance will charter the designated One-Stop
Workforce Development System sites. Performance standards will be established,
including a customer satisfaction index, which will be used to evaluate the effectiveness
of the site in achieving the goals and outcomes established in the WIA's required five
year plan.

ANALYSIS

Government Code section 87300 requires every agency to adopt and promulgate a
conflict of interest code, a document designed to identify those officers, employees,
members, or consultants of any agency who make or participate in the making of agency
decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest
(henceforth called "designated employees" (section 82019)), and assign to those
designated positions appropriate categories of financial disclosure by setting forth the
types of investments, business positions, income, and real property that are reportable by
those designated employees.³ (Section 87302.) Persons designated in a code must
disclose the economic interests that could potentially be affected by their decision
making. (Section 87302.)

As described above, the designation of employees under an agency's conflict of
interest code is left to the discretion of each individual agency and its respective code
reviewing body. (Sections 87300-87310.) Section 82011 indicates that the board of
supervisors is the code reviewing body with respect to the conflict of interest code of any
county agency, other than the board of supervisors, with jurisdiction wholly within the
county.

The Incorporation Page

The first page of your conflict of interest code, the actual code itself, states that
"[s]tatements for all designated employees will be retained by the Stanislaus Economic
Development and Workforce Alliance, Inc." This conflicts with section 87500(j) which
states that:

"...heads of agencies, members of boards or commissions not under a
department of state government or members of boards or commissions not
under the jurisdiction of a local legislative body [file] –one original with
the agency, which shall make and retain a copy and forward the original to
the code reviewing body which shall be the filing officer. In its discretion,

³ In a previous letter to you (Rein Advice Letter, No. A-02-124) we concluded that members of
the Business and Workforce Alliance of Stanislaus County are public officials who must be designated in a
conflict of interest code and file Statements of Economis Interests.

File No. 1-03-266
Page No. 3

the code reviewing body may provide that the original be filed directly
with the code reviewing body and that no copy be retained by the agency."

For those designated employees who are board members, your agency must retain
a copy of the statements and forward the original statements to the code reviewing body.
The code reviewing body for the Alliance would be the Stanislaus County Board of
Supervisors. (Section 82011.)

Appendix A – Designated Positions

Appendix A of your proposed conflict of interest code contains language
exempting "consultants" generally from filing statements of economic interests.
"Consultant," 4 as defined by the Act at regulation 18701(a)(2), is a term of art used to
categorize certain outside service providers as public officials under the Act. In other
words, every consultant is not a "consultant" under the Act. If a service provider meets
the criteria for being a "consultant" as the term is used in regulation 18701(a)(2), then
that person is, by definition, a "public official," and will be required to file a statement of
economic interests.

The language you may wish to use is as follows:

"Consultants shall be included in the list of designated employees and
shall disclose pursuant to the broadest disclosure category in the code
subject to the following limitation:

The executive director may determine in writing that a particular
consultant, although a 'designated position' is hired to perform a range of
duties that is limited in scope and thus is not required to fully comply with
the disclosure requirements in this section. Such written determination

4 The Act's definition of consultant is "an individual who, pursuant to a contract with a state or
local government agency:

(A) Makes a governmental decision whether to:
1. Approve a rate, rule, or regulation;
2. Adopt or enforce a law;
3. Issue, deny, suspend, or revoke any permit, license, application, certificate, approval, order, or
similar authorization or entitlement;
4. Authorize the agency to enter into, modify, or renew a contract provided it is the type of
contract that requires agency approval;
5. Grant agency approval to a contract that requires agency approval and to which the agency is a
party, or to the specifications for such a contract;
6. Grant agency approval to a plan, design, report, study, or similar item;
7. Adopt, or grant agency approval of, policies, standards, or guidelines for the agency, or for any
subdivision thereof; or
(B) Serves in a staff capacity with the agency and in that capacity participates in making a
governmental decision as defined in Regulation 18702.2 or performs the same or substantially all
the same duties for the agency that would otherwise be performed by an individual holding a
position specified in the agency's Conflict of Interest Code under Government Code Section
87302."

File No. I-03-266
Page No. 4

shall include a description of the consultant's duties and based upon that
description, a statement of the extent of disclosure requirements. The
executive director's determination is public record and shall be retained
for public inspection in the same manner and location as this conflict of
interest code."

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Adrianne Korchmaros
Political Reform Consultant

sections.question_synthetic: Does the Stanislaus Economic Development and Workforce Alliance's draft conflict of interest code comply with the Political Reform Act's requirements for designating employees, filing statements, and handling consultants?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC identifies three issues with the draft code: (1) the incorporation page must specify that statements are retained by the agency and forwarded to the code reviewing body (Board of Supervisors); (2) designated employees must file with the agency, which retains a copy and forwards the original to the code reviewing body; and (3) consultants cannot be generally exempted from filing—instead, the executive director may make written determinations limiting specific consultants' disclosure requirements based on the scope of their duties.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: This is an informal advice letter (File No. I-03-266) with no explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections. The document follows a standard FPPC advice letter format with BACKGROUND and ANALYSIS sections. The question and conclusion were synthesized from the letter's content. High confidence in extraction despite lack of formal Q/C sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Stanislaus Economic Development and Workforce Alliance's draft conflict of interest code comply with the Political Reform Act's requirements for designating employees, filing statements, and handling consultants?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC identifies three issues with the draft code: (1) the incorporation page must specify that statements are retained by the agency and forwarded to the code reviewing body (Board of Supervisors); (2) designated employees must file with the agency, which retains a copy and forwards the original to the code reviewing body; and (3) consultants cannot be generally exempted from filing—instead, the executive director may make written determinations limiting specific consultants' disclosure requirements based on the scope of their duties.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to a request for informal assistance regarding a draft conflict of interest code for the Stanislaus Economic Development and Workforce Alliance, identifying three compliance issues related to statement retention and filing procedures, and consultant designation and disclosure requirements.
